claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 6 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin your Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.

**How old is your child?**

Please let me know if they are:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **Between 16 and 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children you'd like to claim for, please tell me all their ages (and their names would be helpful too, so I can keep track).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh yes, I have two children. My daughter Alex is 6 years old, and my son Blake is 3 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Wonderful, thank you for that information! Since both Alex (6) and Blake (3) are under 16 years old, they automatically meet the age requirement for Child Benefit. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Since Alex (6) and Blake (3) are both young children well under 16, they won't be working. So this check is satisfied for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
The next question is about whether the children receive certain benefits in their own right.

**Are Alex and Blake NOT receiving any benefits in their own right** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

Given their young ages (6 and 3), I would expect the answer is "no, they don't receive such benefits" — but I just want to confirm this with you.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, neither of them receive any benefits in their own name. They're just young kids.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's what I expected. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Given that Alex is 6 and Blake is 3, they're far too young to be in any apprenticeship, so this check is easily satisfied.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status. 

**Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **any one** of these applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. **You have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **You have pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3+ continuous months)
4. **You have pre-settled status** and are actively looking for work (can claim as a jobseeker for up to 91 days)
5. **You have pre-settled status** with sufficient resources to support yourself and your family (and not claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit)
6. **You have pre-settled status** and are a student with sufficient resources
7. **You have pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with the right to reside
8. **You are a Crown servant** living abroad (or in certain qualifying countries)

Which of these applies to you? For most people, it's simply option 1 — do you normally live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that's straightforward. You meet the residency requirement as you normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to confirm your responsibility for Alex and Blake.

**Are you responsible for both children?**

You qualify if **any one** of these applies:

1. **You live with the children** (Alex and Blake)
2. **You contribute to their upkeep** (at least the same amount as Child Benefit, through food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents, etc.)
3. **You pay maintenance** under a court order or agreement

Do you live with Alex and Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, both Alex and Blake live at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that confirms you're responsible for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about other potential claimants.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

For example, this could be another parent, a guardian, or another family member. 

- If no one else is claiming, that's straightforward
- If someone else is claiming but you've agreed between yourselves that you should be the one to claim, that's also fine
- If there's a dispute about who should claim, HMRC would need to make a decision

Is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for either of your children?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good to know. Now I need to check about the children's living situation.

**Where are Alex and Blake currently living?**

Are they:
- **In hospital** (for an extended stay)
- **In local authority care**
- **Being fostered**
- **Living normally at home with you** (the usual situation)

I assume they're just living at home with you in Manchester — is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex lives at home with me normally. But Blake has actually been in hospital for a while now - a few months actually.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to hear that Blake has been in hospital. I hope he recovers well.

Since Alex and Blake are in different situations, let me handle them separately.

**For Alex** (living at home normally), I'll continue with that path.

**For Blake** (in hospital), I need to ask a bit more about the duration.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. For Blake, there are special rules about children in hospital.

**How long has Blake been in hospital?**

Here's what you need to know:
- Child Benefit continues for up to **12 weeks** while a child is in hospital
- If there have been multiple hospital stays with gaps of less than 28 days between them, these are added together cumulatively

You mentioned Blake has been in hospital for "a few months." Could you be more specific about how long? Has it been:
- **Less than 12 weeks** (under about 3 months)
- **More than 12 weeks** (over about 3 months)

And has this been one continuous stay, or have there been gaps where he came home?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been more than 3 months - about 13 weeks actually. It's been one continuous stay.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Since Blake has been in hospital for about 13 weeks (more than the 12-week limit), there's an additional requirement I need to check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Since Blake has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks, there's an important exception that could still allow you to claim Child Benefit for him.

**Are you regularly spending money on Blake while he's in hospital?**

This could include things like:
- Buying clothes, toys, or books for him
- Paying for snacks or treats
- Travel costs to visit him
- Any other regular spending for his benefit

If you (or your spouse/civil partner if you live together) are regularly spending money on Blake, you can still receive Child Benefit for him even though he's been in hospital for more than 12 weeks.

Are you regularly spending money on Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I haven't been able to afford to spend money on his needs while he's been in there.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand, and I'm sorry to hear that. Financial situations can be very difficult, especially when a child is in hospital.

Unfortunately, this means that for Blake, the eligibility outcome is different from Alex's.

Let me now complete the assessment for Alex, who is living at home with you normally.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
One final question for Alex:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is Alex fully supported by you (or family) rather than having the council pay for her accommodation or upkeep?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. The council isn't paying anything for Alex. I support her myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's the final piece of information I needed. Let me now provide you with the outcome of your Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment - Results

### Summary

| Child | Age | Eligible? |
|-------|-----|-----------|
| **Alex** | 6 | ✅ **Yes** |
| **Blake** | 3 | ❌ **No** (currently) |

---

### Alex (6 years old) — **ELIGIBLE** ✅

Alex meets all the eligibility requirements:
- She's under 16 years old
- She lives at home with you in Manchester
- You're responsible for her care
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for her
- The local council isn't paying for her accommodation

**Next Steps for Alex:**
1. Apply online or by post using form CH2
2. Provide proof of your identity and Alex's birth certificate
3. Payments are usually made every 4 weeks
4. Be aware: if you or a partner earns over £60,000, the High Income Child Benefit Charge may apply

🔗 Apply here: [gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

---

### Blake (3 years old) — **NOT ELIGIBLE** (currently) ❌

I'm sorry, but Blake doesn't currently qualify because:
- He has been in hospital for more than **12 weeks** (approximately 13 weeks)
- You are not regularly spending money on him during his hospital stay

**Important:** This situation can change:
- **If Blake comes home** — you would become eligible to claim for him again
- **If you start regularly spending money on Blake** (even small amounts for clothes, toys, treats, etc.) — you could become eligible
- If Blake leaves hospital and then returns within 28 days, the time will count cumulatively towards the 12-week limit

---

I know this is a difficult time with Blake being in hospital. If your circumstances change, please do check again or contact HMRC directly to discuss your situation. They may be able to advise on any exceptions that could apply.

Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify?

reasonings: Alex (6 years old) meets all eligibility criteria: (1) She is under 16, so automatically qualifies on age. (2) She is not working and does not receive benefits in her own right. (3) She is not in an apprenticeship. (4) You meet residency requirements as you normally live in the UK. (5) You are responsible for Alex as she lives with you. (6) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for her. (7) She lives normally at home with you. (8) The local council is not paying for her accommodation or maintenance. Therefore, you are ELIGIBLE to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake (3 years old) meets most eligibility criteria, but unfortunately falls short due to the hospital rule: (1) He is under 16, so qualifies on age. (2) He is not working and does not receive benefits in his own right. (3) He is not in an apprenticeship. (4) You meet residency requirements. (5) You are responsible for Blake. (6) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him. However, (7) Blake has been in hospital for approximately 13 weeks, which exceeds the 12-week limit. (8) You are not regularly spending money on Blake while he is in hospital. Under the rules, Child Benefit stops after 12 weeks in hospital unless you are regularly spending money on the child. Therefore, you are currently INELIGIBLE to claim Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible for Child Benefit for one of your two children. Alex qualifies fully as she lives at home with you and meets all requirements. Blake, unfortunately, does not currently qualify because he has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks and you are not regularly spending money on him during his stay. If Blake's circumstances change (e.g., he comes home, or you begin regularly spending money on him), you may become eligible to claim for him again.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child's cumulative time in hospital or residential accommodation exceeded 12 weeks?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks in hospital/residential accommodation. CUMULATIVE RULE: If child returns within 28 days of leaving, time counts towards 12-week limit. Multiple stays within 28 days are added together.

criteria.calculation_method: Sum all hospital/residential stays. If gap between stays is less than 28 days, count both stays towards cumulative total. If gap is 28+ days, reset counter.
criteria.example: 10 weeks hospital + 15 day gap + 3 weeks hospital = 13 weeks cumulative (exceeds limit)

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Are you regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital/residential accommodation?
response.help_text: Exception to 12-week rule if you regularly spend money on child. For hospital abroad, only if they went abroad for treatment and you're back in UK. Partner's spending counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

response.reason: Child has been in hospital or residential accommodation for more than 12 weeks and you are not regularly spending money on them
response.guidance: Child Benefit stops after 12 weeks in hospital or residential accommodation, unless you regularly spend money on them. If child returns within 28 days of leaving, the time counts towards the 12-week limit. Contact HMRC to check if exceptions apply. Partner's spending only counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (6 years old) meets all eligibility criteria: (1) She is under 16, so automatically qualifies on age. (2) She is not working and does not receive benefits in her own right. (3) She is not in an apprenticeship. (4) You meet residency requirements as you normally live in the UK. (5) You are responsible for Alex as she lives with you. (6) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for her. (7) She lives normally at home with you. (8) The local council is not paying for her accommodation or maintenance. Therefore, you are ELIGIBLE to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake (3 years old) meets most eligibility criteria, but unfortunately falls short due to the hospital rule: (1) He is under 16, so qualifies on age. (2) He is not working and does not receive benefits in his own right. (3) He is not in an apprenticeship. (4) You meet residency requirements. (5) You are responsible for Blake. (6) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him. However, (7) Blake has been in hospital for approximately 13 weeks, which exceeds the 12-week limit. (8) You are not regularly spending money on Blake while he is in hospital. Under the rules, Child Benefit stops after 12 weeks in hospital unless you are regularly spending money on the child. Therefore, you are currently INELIGIBLE to claim Child Benefit for Blake.

response.overall_reasoning: You are eligible for Child Benefit for one of your two children. Alex qualifies fully as she lives at home with you and meets all requirements. Blake, unfortunately, does not currently qualify because he has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks and you are not regularly spending money on him during his stay. If Blake's circumstances change (e.g., he comes home, or you begin regularly spending money on him), you may become eligible to claim for him again.
